title: FIA-Compliant Hobby Kart 3D Design
summary: A team of three engineering students designed and modelled a complete FIA-compliant hobby kart in SolidWorks, producing a fully constrained 32-part assembly with technical drawings and bill of materials. The final assembly achieved 102.7 kg mass with an estimated production cost of €2,900.
details:
The project objective was to design and model a FIA-compliant hobby kart using SolidWorks with focus on structural integrity, component interoperability, and maintainability. Before detailed design commenced, the team evaluated five candidate vehicle concepts using a weighted decision matrix scoring cost (40%), project scope (40%), and time (20%). The go-kart concept scored 4.0 out of 5, outperforming alternatives including drones, skateboards, e-bicycles, and aircraft. The deliverables comprised a fully constrained SolidWorks assembly encompassing chassis construction, suspension layout, steering system, and braking system, together with engineering drawings conforming to ISO 2768-f standards.

Work was distributed across three subsystem owners using a responsibility-assignment framework. Kristjan Sarapuu owned the chassis frame, steering system and column, simplified engine representation, and wheels. Mikk Eke Kalamees designed the driver seat, drive system, pedals and mounts, floor plate, and body panels. Hendrik Tiirats was responsible for the complete braking system including callipers, disc, pads, and master cylinder, plus wheels, hubs, and tyres. Every component was modelled as a discrete SolidWorks part and mated into the top-level assembly with fully defined geometric constraints.

The chassis was designed as a welded steel tube structure complying with FIA stiffness and safety requirements at an estimated cost of €750. The powertrain featured a simplified four-stroke engine representation (assumed Rotax-equivalent, €650) inclusive of clutch and primary drive. The suspension and braking subsystem included a rear axle, complete brake system with AISI 1045 steel disc (Ø220/Ø160, 12 mm thickness, 10× M6 bolt pattern, 1.42 kg), callipers, pads, rims, and tyres totalling €850. Safety elements including seat harness, bumpers, and fuel tank accounted for €400, while steering components, drive chain, bearings, and miscellaneous hardware added €250.

All 32 unique part types were documented with engineering drawings to ISO 2768-f general tolerancing. Key components included front tyres (rubber, Ø140 bore / Ø260 OD / Ø280 section, 4.58 kg), rear axle (alloy steel, 1040 mm length, Ø56, 3 mm wall thickness, 3.68 kg), steering tie-rod ends (alloy steel, 170 mm length, 0.23 kg), and brake discs. The project deepened understanding of subsystem interdependencies, particularly geometric constraints linking steering geometry, wheel hub sizing, and brake disc fitment. Structural FEA and detailed load calculations were deliberately scoped out; the 3D model provides a foundation for future stress analysis or prototype fabrication. The complete assembly is publicly accessible via the 3DExperience platform.

specs.value: Ø140 bore / Ø260 OD / Ø280 section
